A raise would be reckless and unnecessary. Fold to preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

I am UTG in a 4-handed game with Qs 5s. While suited queens have some value, Q5s is generally too weak to open from this position, especially with Claude sitting on a short stack of less than 15 big blinds. Opening here would likely commit me to calling a shove from Claude or facing 3-bets from Minni and Elon with a hand that has poor playability post-flop. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to maintain my chip lead.

6-3 offsuit is one of the weakest possible starting hands in poker. Despite being in a favorable button position, my stack is already relatively short at 1475 chips, and I need to be selective about which hands I invest in. Flash has already folded, and with Minni and Elon in the blinds, there's no particular advantage to playing this trash hand. I should preserve my chips and wait for a stronger hand to make a stand.

Heads-up from the small blind with K♠ T♦ — a playable, slightly above-average hand heads-up. Pot is 150 and I only need 50 to call, but calling would play this hand out of position for a marginal hand and give Elon cheap odds to see a flop. Elon has shown frequent preflop aggression, so I want initiative and fold equity. I’ll open-raise to a standard 3x-ish size (300 total) — large enough to force folds and charge speculative hands, but small enough to keep the pot manageable with deep stacks. Plan: take the lead on most flops (c-bet when favorable), re-evaluate vs a 3-bet or strong action on the flop/turn.

Preflop in BB facing a standard raise to 300 (about 3BB) from SB Minni after UTG and Button folds. My hand 3s8h is uncoordinated trash with no high card value, poor playability postflop, and dominated against any reasonable raising range. Pot odds to call 200 into ~600 are decent (~3:1), but equity realization is terrible even if I flop something, as SB ranges are wide but still crush this hand. No reason to raise or defend with this; preserve stack for better spots. Standard fold.

6-10 offsuit is a weak hand from UTG (first to act), one of the worst positions at the table. With a short stack of 1475 (approximately 14.75 big blinds), I need to be very selective about the hands I play from early position. There are three opponents yet to act, so the probability of facing aggression is high. This hand doesn't meet the threshold for early position play given my stack situation - I need to preserve chips for premium hands like pairs and strong broadway cards. A fold here is the disciplined play to maintain fold equity and wait for better spots.

I’m on the button with Q♠9♣ — a playable, marginal hand but much stronger in position. The pot is only 150 and the amount to call is 100. I want to take the initiative and apply pressure to the blinds, so I’ll open-raise to 300 (≈3× the BB). This size gives good fold equity, keeps my SPR healthy given my 13.7k stack against Elon (SB 8.15k) and Flash (BB 16.5k), and lets me play postflop with position. Plan: if both fold I pick up the blinds; if one or both call I will c-bet on dry/flop textures and play pot-control on connected or dangerous boards. If I face a large 3-bet (a substantial shove or a size well above a standard 3-bet), Q9o is usually beat — I’ll fold to heavy aggression.

Preflop in SB facing a button raise to 3BB from Minni, who has been aggressive in previous hands with frequent raises. 89o is a speculative connector with good playability, capable of flopping strong hands like straights or two-pair. Pot odds are favorable: calling 250 more into 450 makes the pot 700, requiring about 26% equity, which 89o achieves against a wide button opening range. Position on BB post-flop allows control. Folding would be too tight in SB; 3-betting is suboptimal with an offsuit non-premium hand. Calling keeps the pot multi-way potentially and realizes equity well.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 2c, which is a very weak hand. Minni raised from the Button and Elon called from the Small Blind. Although the pot odds are 3.5:1 (200 to call into a 700 pot), 92o plays poorly post-flop, and I would be out of position against two active players. With Claude being extremely short-stacked (less than 15 BBs), there is no need to risk chips with a bottom-tier hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Preflop called with 89o from SB vs button raise, which hit top pair (9s) with weak 8 kicker and gutshot straight draw to the 7 on a dry 2-6-9 rainbow board. Heads-up vs Minni who folded Flash out preflop. First to act OOP. Board favors my range somewhat, but betting extracts value from her wide button opening range (worse pairs, draws like 78/T7/57, floats). Protects against overcards and straight draws. Checking risks giving free cards to improvers or facing a bet where I have to decide. Bet 2/3 pot (450) balances value/thin value/protection, keeps pot manageable with my 7900 stack (128BB), induces calls from dominated hands while setting up fold equity.

I have middle pair (9) with a strong Q kicker on a dry 2♠‑9♥‑6♦ flop. Elon’s small c‑bet (450) represents a wide range (misses, floats, some 9x, occasional sets/overpairs). I’m in position so I prefer pot control and to realize equity rather than bloating the pot with a marginal hand. Raising to 900 risks a larger 3‑bet from stronger hands and folding out many of his bluffs; folding is too tight given my showdown value and the pot odds. I’ll call 450, see the turn, and re‑evaluate — continue for value against turn checks/small bets and be prepared to fold to large aggression or dangerous turn runouts.

On the turn, the board is 2s-9h-6d-5h. Holding 8h9d for top pair with an 8 kicker, which remains a strong hand heads-up against Minni's button calling range. The turn 5h adds heart flush draws and possible straight draws (e.g., 3-4-5-6-7 gutshots or wheel draws with 3-4), increasing the drawing possibilities. My flop bet was called, confirming interest, but continuing aggression extracts value from her worse pairs (e.g., 97, T9, 96), overcards, and draws while protecting against overcards or runner-runner threats. Checking risks giving a free card to improvers or facing a raise without initiative. With deep stacks (7450 into 1600 pot), a pot-sized bet of 1000 builds the pot for river value if checked through or called, balancing value and fold equity against her calling station tendencies observed in prior hands (e.g., multiple calls on scary boards). This maximizes EV by denying equity and charging draws.

I have top pair (9s) with a decent Q kicker on a dry 2♠‑9♥‑6♦‑5♥ board. I called Elon's small flop c‑bet to keep his range wide and use position; his 1,000 turn bet into ~2,600 is a small/medium sizing and is not strongly polarized — it can include bluffs/missed floats, thin value (9x) and the occasional set or 5x. My hand has good showdown value but is vulnerable to A9/K9 and sets, so I don't want to bloat the pot by raising and risk getting crushed. Calling preserves pot control, gets to see the river with position, and gives me the ability to fold to heavy aggression or extract value from weaker hands. I will call the 1,000 and re‑evaluate on the river based on the runout and Elon's action.

River board 2s-9h-6d-5h-3d with 8h9d for top pair (9s) and weak 8 kicker, OOP vs Minni's button calling range after calling flop and turn bets. The connected board (2-3-5-6-9) screams danger: villain can easily have straights (esp. with a 4x for 23456), sets (22/66/99), two-pairs (e.g., 95s, 63s, 25s), or better pairs (T+, J+). Turn 5h and river 3d completed many draws that were live on flop/turn (e.g., 34/47/78 gutshots, wheel draws). Minni's calls suggest strength or float with position, unlikely pure air given board texture and two barrels. Betting risks check-raise all-in (her stack 11975 > pot) or call from better; thin value from worse pairs/draws not worth it with deep stacks. Checking: induces bluffs from missed draws/overcards, showdowns best hand if ahead (vs. overpairs? unlikely), or faces bet where pot odds ~2:1 allow hero-call hero-fold against polarized range. History shows Minni calls stations but folds to aggression sometimes; check maximizes EV by avoiding spew, denying unnecessary equity realization, targets long-term stack growth in tournament.

I have top pair (9♣) with a good Q kicker on a very dry 2♠‑9♥‑6♦‑5♥‑3♦ board. Elon bet the flop and the turn but checked the river — that line looks like pot control or shows weakness (floats/overcards/medium pairs) more often than a value-polarized river check. Betting here both extracts thin value from worse hands (we beat many Kx/Ax/weak pairs and some 9x with worse kickers) and prices out hands that want to realize equity. I don’t want to bloat the pot and get raised off against sets/A9/K9, so I choose a medium sizing to maximize fold equity and value while keeping a comfortable fold option if Elon check‑raises. I’ll bet ~1/2 pot (1,800). If he folds I take it down; if he makes a large check‑raise I’ll fold to preserve my stack; if he calls we go to showdown with a hand that often wins.

Facing a 1800 bet into 5400 pot on a scary river board (2s9h6d5h3d) with 89o for top pair weak kicker. Minni's button raise preflop followed by calls to two streets OOP against aggression indicates strength, likely straights (any 4x for 23456, 47 for 34567), two pairs (e.g., 95s, 63s), sets (22/33/66/99), or better pairs that beat my TPGK. Bluffs possible (missed heart/diamond draws, overcards), but her history as an aggressive raiser who calls stations suggests value-heavy range when betting after check. Pot odds ~3:1 require ~25% equity; I estimate <25% vs her likely range (e.g., 4x hands crush me, worse hands unlikely after calls). Calling risks 1800 (28% stack) with vulnerable hand in tournament where survival > thin calls. Folding preserves stack for better spots, consistent with prior check to realize equity or induce bluffs without paying off.

Qc3d UTG in a 4-handed game is a very weak, offsuit, non-connective hand and is easily dominated by many hands that will open/3‑bet from later positions. Limping (calling 100) is passive and invites raises; opening to 200 risks isolating against a short-stacked big blind who could shove or the button who often 3‑bets. With a healthy stack (15575) I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot in position. Fold to avoid difficult postflop decisions with a poor hand.

Folded to me on the button holding pocket 2s, a speculative hand that plays well post-flop in position. Great stealing spot with dead money in the pot (150) and position on both blinds. SB (Flash) is deep (164BB), BB (Claude) is short (~14BB) and may shove wide for value. 22 has ~50% equity vs typical shoving ranges and set-mining potential if called. Standard to raise to 3x BB for value, isolation, and fold equity. Minni already folded UTG.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d 6h, a weak offsuit hand. Elon opened from the Button for 300. While I have a large stack, 96o is not a hand I want to play out of position, especially with a very short-stacked player (Claude) still to act in the Big Blind who could easily shove. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my chip lead.

6-9 offsuit is a weak hand with minimal value. While the pot odds are decent (approximately 3:1 requiring ~30% equity), 6-9o likely has only 25-30% equity against Elon's wide button-raising range. More importantly, I'll be out of position for the entire hand after the flop, which is a significant disadvantage. My stack is also getting shallow at 1375 chips, and calling 200 would leave me with only 1175 remaining. The risk/reward isn't favorable enough to justify calling with such a weak hand from the worst position. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities with stronger holdings.

UTG with 9s3d offsuit is one of the worst possible starting hands. No high card value, no suited potential for flush draws, large gap prevents straight draws. Position is the worst at a 4-handed table, and with deep stacks and aggressive opponents like Minni who frequently raises, entering the pot here commits chips with minimal equity. Pot odds to limp are poor (limping UTG invites raises), and raising is suicidal. Clear fold to preserve stack for premium hands in later position or better spots. Long-term EV positive to fold trash.

I am on the button with a strong broadway hand, King-Queen offsuit. Claude in the small blind is very short-stacked with only 1,325 chips, while Minni in the big blind is the other large stack. I will make a standard opening raise to 300 to pick up the blinds or to play a pot in position. If Claude shoves for his remaining stack, I will likely call given my hand strength and the pot odds.

QK (Queen-King) is a premium starting hand, ranking in the top 10 hands in poker. With a relatively short stack of 1325 chips (~13 BB), this is an ideal situation to commit chips. Flash's raise to 300 from the button is standard, and I'm in the small blind with excellent equity. Given my stack depth and hand strength, going all-in is the optimal play here. This move: (1) commits with a strong hand, (2) eliminates post-flop complexity where my short stack limits options, (3) applies maximum pressure on Flash and forces Minni to make a decision with their entire range, and (4) has strong expected value against Flash's likely opening range. With a short stack in a tournament, you want to get it in with premium hands like QK, not let blinds whittle you away.

I’m in the big blind with 6♣7♣ facing a button open to 300 and a small‑blind 3‑bet to 1325 (he’s left with only ~50). Pot is 1725 and it costs me 1225 to continue. With deep effective stacks vs the button and a very short SB, 6♣7♣ has good post‑flop playability and reasonable equity — calling is cheap (~8% of my stack) and keeps options open. 4‑betting here with a marginal suited connector is unnecessary risk; folding is too tight given the price and potential to realize equity, and calling lets me either see a flop multi‑way (or isolate if Flash folds) and fold to heavy action if I miss. Plan: call to see a flop, pursue draws or strong connections, and fold to significant aggression on dry/unfavorable boards.

I opened the action to 300 with King-Queen offsuit, a strong broadway hand. Claude, who is extremely short-stacked, raised to 1,325, effectively going all-in. Minni called the raise from the big blind. The pot currently stands at 2,950, and it costs me 1,025 more to call. Given the excellent pot odds (nearly 3:1) and the strength of my hand, folding is not an option. I also have position on Minni for the rest of the hand. Calling closes the pre-flop action and allows me to see a flop and evaluate the board before committing more chips against Minni's stack.

With 50 chips remaining in a 3,975 chip pot and QK on an 8h-6s-7d flop, I'm extremely short-stacked. I have solid equity with two overcards and a gutshot straight draw (any 5 or 9). Checking allows me to see additional cards without risking my remaining chips unless forced by an opponent's bet. This maximizes my expected value given the massive pot-to-stack ratio and my hand's drawing potential.

I have 6♣7♣ on 8♥-6♠-7♦ — that's two pair (7s & 6s). Very strong on this flop vs a wide calling range from Flash. Claude is effectively a tiny short‑stack (≈50) who checked, so he can't meaningfully pressure me further; the real extraction is from Flash. The board has some danger (sets like 66/77/88 and straight combos that include both a 5 and a 9 or 9+10), but those are less common than the one‑pair/overcard hands and draws that will call. Betting here both builds the pot for value and denies free cards (especially 5/9 turn outs). I’ll bet for value and protection — sizing to about 2/3 pot to charge worse pairs/overcards and draws while still getting called. If I face a large raise I’ll re‑evaluate (sets are possible and I can fold to extreme aggression); if called I’ll assess the turn and continue for value on safe cards or pot‑control on dangerous cards.

On a board of 8h-6s-7d, my King-Queen offsuit has missed completely. This board is extremely coordinated and favors a range that contains middle connectors and sets, which Minni (Big Blind) could easily have after calling the pre-flop raise. Minni's lead of 2,600 into the multi-way pot (where Claude is effectively all-in) is a show of significant strength. With only two overcards and no straight or flush draws, I have very little equity to continue. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

With 50 chips remaining and already committed 1,325 chips pre-flop, I'm pot-committed. QK on an 8h-6s-7d board gives me two overcards and a gutshot straight draw (any 5 or 9). Against Minni's likely range, I have decent equity. The pot odds are exceptional (calling 50 into 6,575+), and folding forfeits my entire pre-flop investment. This is a clear all-in call with a hand that has drawing potential and equity.

Three-handed on the button with 7♠6♦ (offsuit connector). In 3-handed play my opening range should be wide — this hand has good postflop playability and benefits a lot from position. I want to take the initiative, isolate the blinds and avoid a multiway limp-pot, so I’ll open to a standard ~3x BB (300) rather than just limp or min-raise. Stacks are deep enough to continue postflop; plan is to c-bet favorable flops and fold to heavy aggression if I miss and face a large 3‑bet.

Pocket Aces is the best starting hand in poker. Minni's button raise to 300 is standard but likely wide from the button given history of frequent raises. With deep stacks and out of position, I need to 3-bet for value and to isolate, reducing multiway chances. Flash in BB may fold or call light, but AA crushes typical calling ranges. Sizing to 1000 total (about 3.3x the raise) balances protection, value, and fold equity while keeping pot manageable OOP. Folding or calling is -EV.

In a 3-handed game, AQo is a premier hand. Elon's 3-bet from the small blind is significant, especially given his stack size of 5,600. Calling the 900 to close the action or see what the button (Minni) does is a solid play. It keeps the pot manageable while allowing me to utilize my post-flop skills. AQo is too strong to fold and 4-betting might force out hands I dominate or commit me against a potentially stronger range from the short stack.

Fold. Elon 3-bet to 1000 and Flash called, turning this into a 3-bet multiway pot. 7♠6♦ offsuit has very limited equity and post‑flop playability against a 3‑bet range plus a calling range. Calling 700 into a 2300 pot (pot after my call ~3000) requires ~23% equity — I don't expect 7‑6o to have that versus two narrower, aggressive ranges, and I lose fold equity and face difficult decisions on many runouts. A min‑4bet to 1700 would be a weak, non‑credible squeeze and a larger 4bet or shove is too risky with this hand. With the shortish effective stack behind Elon and the deeper BB calling range, conserving chips for better spots is the correct line.

Pocket Aces remain very strong on this 3s-6s-Qd flop, an overpair dominating most of Flash's BB defending range against my preflop 3-bet (e.g., suited broadways like KQs, AQs, JTs; mid pairs like 88-JJ; suited connectors 78s, 45s that may have hit two-pair or straight draws). The spade flush draw is live, but Qd provides little help to straight draws or sets (33/66/QQ unlikely in his call range). As OOP aggressor, betting builds the pot for value from worse pairs/draws while charging equity and denying him free cards. Pot is 2300, my stack 5600 (SPR ~2.4), so a 2/3 pot bet of 1500 protects my hand, gets value from ~top 10% of his range, and sets up turn shove if called. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or inducing bluffs, but betting maintains initiative and leverage.

On a flop of Qd-6s-3s, I have top pair, top kicker (AQo). Pre-flop, Elon (the small blind) 3-bet into a button open and my big blind, indicating a strong range. However, with the current board, my hand is extremely strong. Elon has led for 1,500 into a 2,300 pot, leaving him with only 3,100 behind. With an SPR (stack-to-pot ratio) this low, I am committed to this hand. Calling is the best play here to allow Elon to continue bluffing with hands like AK or flush draws, while I am never folding to a shove on the turn. A raise here might force out his bluffs, whereas calling keeps his range wide.

AA remains an overpair on a 3s6sQd7h board, ahead of Flash's BB defending range against my 3-bet (suited broadways like KQs/JTs, mid-pairs 88-JJ, spade draws like AsXs/Ksxs, suited connectors like 45s/78s). Flop call shows strength or draw; turn 7h bricks the spade flush draw but completes 4-5 straight draws and adds gutshots. With pot 5300 and my stack 4100 (SPR ~0.77), we're pot-committed. OOP aggressor, betting all-in extracts max value from Qx, worse pairs (99-JJ), incomplete draws, and applies max pressure to floats/bluffs while denying further equity realization. Checking risks a check-raise from strong hands or free river card to draws; jamming balances thin value/protection and is +EV given AA's equity vs range (~55-65% vs typical calling combos). Prior history shows Flash calls light but folds to aggression (e.g., Hand 14, 61). Shove maximizes EV.

On a turn board of 3s-6s-Qd-7h, I hold top pair, top kicker (AQo). Elon has moved all-in for 4,100 into a pot of 5,300. Given the pot odds (calling 4,100 to win a total pot of 13,500), I need roughly 30% equity to justify a call. While Elon's 3-bet pre-flop and aggression on the flop and turn could indicate a hand like AA, KK, or QQ, his range also includes KQ, QJ, and flush draws (like AsKs or AsJs) that I currently beat. Given the strength of my hand relative to the board and the favorable pot odds, I must call the all-in bet.

Preflop on the button 3-handed with Qc9c, suited connectors with good playability and straight/flush potential. Excellent position to realize equity multiway or take initiative. Opponents have deep stacks, but button allows control postflop. Standard raise to 3x BB to steal blinds or build pot. History shows aggressive opponents, so raising thins field and applies pressure. Folding would be too tight 3-handed.

I am in the Small Blind with Qd 7s. Elon, the button, has raised to 300. While the pot odds are roughly 1.8 to 1 to call, Q7o is a weak hand to play out of position against a button raise, especially with the Big Blind (Minni) still left to act and holding a massive stack. After losing a significant pot to Elon in the previous hand, I need to be more selective with the hands I play to preserve my remaining 8,475 chips and wait for a better spot to recoup my losses.

I'm in the big blind with A♣K♣ facing a button open to 300 (SB folded). Pot is 450 and it's 200 to call. AKo suited is a top-tier hand heads-up — folding is out and simply calling surrenders initiative and lets Elon see flops in position for cheap. I want to 3-bet for value and to isolate, denying equity to speculative hands and giving myself fold equity. I choose a standard 3x sizing: raise to 900 total. That size is big enough to charge draws and weaker broadways, still small relative to deep stacks (keeps future options), and sets up profitable postflop play. If Elon 4-bets hugely I will re-evaluate (I may fold to a polarizing shove but will continue vs realistic 4-bet sizes); if called I'll proceed with standard c-bet/board-dependent strategies to extract value and protect my hand.

Facing a 3-bet from Minni in the BB after my BTN open to 300. Qc9c is playable suited connectors with position, but Minni's 3-bet range is likely strong (QQ+, AKo/s, maybe some bluffs or suited broadways). Equity vs a tight value-heavy range is around 25-30%, not enough for the 600 call into 1250 pot (needing ~32%). 4-betting risks bloating pot OOP postflop with marginal hand. Folding preserves stack for better spots; Minni aggressive but this spot polarized toward value. History shows Minni raises light but BB defense vs BTN often tighter.

I am on the button with 43o, which is one of the weakest starting hands. Although we are three-handed and the button is a prime stealing position, both Minni and Elon have been active and aggressive throughout the tournament. With a stack of over 84 big blinds, there is no need to take an unnecessary risk with a bottom-tier hand. I will fold and wait for a more favorable spot.

Heads-up from the small blind with Q♠2♣ — a marginal hand but in heads-up play it's correct to take the initiative and open a wide range. A minimum raise to 200 applies pressure to the big blind, gives me fold equity to steal the 100 BB pot, and keeps my risk small relative to both stacks. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet (this hand has poor playability OOP vs strong aggression), but if Elon just calls I'll proceed cautiously out of position — c-bet favorable textures, protect my hand when appropriate, and give up to clear strength. Limping (calling 50) would invite disadvantaged postflop spots; folding is too tight in heads-up. Raise to 200 to try to steal or take the initiative while preserving chips against heavy resistance.

AcTc is a premium hand OOP vs a min-raise from the SB, which has a wide range including many dominated Ax hands, offsuit broadways, and suited connectors. Excellent pot odds but calling OOP allows SB to realize equity cheaply with worse. 3-betting applies pressure, builds pot when ahead, and takes initiative. Minni frequently raises wide preflop and has folded to 3-bets in similar spots (e.g., Hand 86). Raise to 600 total (6x BB size), a standard 3-bet size vs min-raise. Blocks AA/AK, good postflop playability with suited Ace.

I opened to 200 to take the initiative heads-up, but Elon 3-bet to 600. Q♠2♣ is a weak, poorly playable hand out of position and is often dominated by a 3-bettor's range. Calling an extra 400 only gets ~3:1 on a spot where my postflop equity and playability are poor, and a 4-bet bluff here would be too loose and invite difficult decisions. Elon has shown aggressive 3-betting tendencies, so I respect the raise and conserve chips for better, positionally favorable situations.

I open-raise to 300 (3x BB). Rationale: On the button in a 3-handed game K♥3♠ is a marginal holding but very playable with position and deep stacks. Raising leverages position and fold equity to pick up the blinds instead of letting Flash see a cheap flop. A 3BB open is standard and forces opponents to pay to see a flop or reveal strength. Elon (SB) has shown aggression preflop, so I’ll be prepared to fold to a large 3-bet — K3o is often dominated and not worth bloat — but if I get a single call I can C-bet favorable textures (Kx or dry boards) and play pot-control on coordinated or K-dangerous boards. Size keeps pressure while preserving most of my stack (18125), so I raise to 300.

7c4h is one of the worst possible starting hands, completely uncoordinated, low cards, offsuit. Facing a button raise preflop from Minni, who likely has a wide range but still better than this trash. As SB, I'd be out of position postflop if I call or play. Pot odds are poor (250 to call into 450 pot ~36% equity needed, but this hand has <20% vs typical raising range). Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament play, aligning with tight-aggressive strategy to maximize long-term EV.

I am holding 7-5 offsuit in the big blind. Minni, the chip leader, has opened from the button to 300. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win 450), 7-5 offsuit is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position. Defending such a wide range against an active chip leader will lead to difficult post-flop situations and chip attrition. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

A2 offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability and easily dominated postflop. On the button with deep stacks, facing a small call of 100 into 150 pot, the implied odds aren't sufficient given the offsuit nature and low kicker. Opponents like Minni and Flash have shown aggression in past hands, making a fold prudent to preserve stack for premium spots. No strong reason to speculate here.

K9o is a strong hand for a heads-up situation from the small blind. Since Elon folded on the button, it is just me and Minni (the big blind). I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop. If Minni calls, K9o has enough high-card value and playability to proceed, even though I will be out of position for the rest of the hand.

Facing a small‑blind open to 300 while I’m in the big blind with A♥9♥. Pot is 400 and it’s only 200 to call (300 total committed). A9s is strong enough to defend — suited Ace, good playability — and Flash’s SB opens look fairly wide. Calling keeps the pot manageable while letting me realize equity and see a flop; 3‑betting OOP would bloat the pot and create difficult post‑flop spots against a wide calling range. I’ll call (commit a total of 300) and re‑evaluate on the flop: look to c‑bet or barre l when I hit or have strong draws, fold or proceed cautiously to heavy resistance.

On the flop of Ac-Ts-4c, I have King-high. This board heavily favors a pre-flop caller's range or an aggressor who is likely to hold an Ace. As the pre-flop raiser, I should maintain aggression with a continuation bet to represent an Ace or a strong Ten, especially since there is only one opponent. A bet of around half the pot is standard here to apply pressure and potentially win the pot immediately.

I have top pair (A♥9♥) on A‑T‑4 rainbow versus a small, in‑position c‑bet of 300 into a ~900 pot. Flash opened preflop from the SB and their flop bet is small — likely a wide continuation range that includes worse A‑x, broadways, and floats. Calling keeps the pot manageable OOP, allows me to realize equity, and gets value from worse aces and hands that will bluff/float. Raising OOP would bloat the pot and create difficult decisions on later streets against an in‑position opponent. Plan: call the 300, evaluate the turn — extract value or fold to heavy aggression depending on the card and opponent’s line.

The turn brings a second four, pairing the board. Having called my flop continuation bet, Minni likely has at least a piece of this board, such as a Ten or a weak Ace. My King-high has very little showdown value and no significant draw. Betting again is unlikely to force a fold from an Ace or a Ten on this board. I will check to control the pot size and see how Minni responds.

Flash opened wide from the SB and C‑bet the flop small, then checked the paired turn — showing weakness or a marginal hand. I hold top pair (A9♥) with a decent kicker on A‑T‑4‑4; this is likely ahead of a large portion of Flash’s range (worse A‑x, some Tx, floats). Betting for value and protection is the right line OOP: I want to charge worse aces and thin value hands while keeping the pot controllable. I’ll make a half‑pot bet to extract value without bloating the pot out of position. If I get called I’ll reevaluate the river; a big check‑raise should be respected as it likely indicates a set or two‑pair.

On the turn, the board pairs with the Four of Hearts. My King-high has no showdown value and no draw to improve. Minni called my flop continuation bet, indicating she likely has a piece of the board (an Ace, a Ten, or a Four). When I checked the turn, she lead out with a 600 bet into a 1200 pot. Given her range and the fact that I have zero equity to continue, calling is unprofitable and bluff-raising is too risky against an opponent who has already shown interest in the hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 7-2 offsuit, which is widely considered the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With a stack of over 77 big blinds, there is no strategic reason to attempt a steal or defend this hand against active opponents like Minni and Elon. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I'm in the small blind with 3♠9♣ — a weak, offsuit connector with little playability OOP. The pot is 150 and it costs me 50 more to see the flop (completing my SB to 100), so calling gives ~4:1 pot odds to see a flop. Raising to 200 is a possible steal, but Elon (BB) has shown frequent preflop aggression and will often 3‑bet or defend; I don't want to face a 3‑bet or play a bloated pot out of position with this hand. Folding is too nitty given the cheap price. Plan: call the extra 50 (total committed 100), see the flop, and play straightforwardly — fold to significant aggression unless I hit a meaningful pair or draw, or try small pot‑control bluffs only on very favorable runouts.

In the big blind facing a small blind limp after the button folds, with 6c7c suited connectors, checking is optimal. This hand plays well post-flop in a multiway pot or heads-up, offering strong implied odds if we hit draws or pairs. Raising risks playing out of position against a calling range that may include dominated hands or monsters, while a check realizes equity cheaply. Minni's limp suggests a wide range, and our deep stacks (13250 effective) favor seeing flops with speculative hands like this.

I flatted preflop with 3♠9♣ intending to see a cheap flop and play straightforwardly. The flop K♥ 6♦ 2♥ misses my hand completely — no pair, no useful straight or flush draw (only backdoor possibilities that are negligible). Elon checked both preflop and the flop, which shows some weakness, but I’m out of position and have virtually no equity or showdown value. With deep stacks and a small pot (200), the correct, low-variance line is to exercise pot control: check and give up to any meaningful bet. If Elon checks back I’ll see the turn for free and reassess; if he bets I will fold unless the sizing is tiny and I pick up a specific reason to continue. Action: check to preserve stack and avoid getting bluffed off later streets with an empty hand.

Flop board is K♥6♦2♥, giving me middle pair (6s) with a 7 kicker and suited backdoor clubs. Minni's SB limp-check suggests a wide, weak range including Ax offsuit, low pairs, suited connectors, or heart draws. Deep effective stacks (13k) favor aggressive play OOP to deny equity to her draws (hearts, straight draws like 34/45) and extract value from worse pairs/top pair weak kicker. Betting small builds the pot while maintaining fold equity against air/bluffs. A check risks giving free cards to overcards/draws or facing a check-raise. Half-pot sizing balances value/protection without overcommitting.

I completed preflop to see a cheap flop with 3♠9♣. The K♥6♦2♥ flop misses me entirely — no pair, no real draws — and I’m out of position heads‑up. Elon leads for 100 into a 300 pot; calling 100 to win a 400 pot requires ~25% equity. I have ~6 outs (any 3 or 9) → roughly 24% to hit by the river, and many of those made hands will still be second/third pair and often dominated by Elon’s c‑betting range (Kx, Ax, overpairs, broadways). Raising OOP as a bluff with no blockers is a thin, high-variance play. Given stack depth and the marginal EV here, the correct, low‑variance line is to fold and preserve chips for better spots.

I have A♠T♥ on the button in a three‑handed pot with deep stacks and position on both blinds — this is a hand I want to play aggressively. ATo has good blocker value to the nut ace, solid Broadway potential, and plays very well post‑flop in position. I will raise to 600 total to take the initiative, apply pressure to the blinds, and narrow their calling ranges (discouraging weak defenses from BB and forcing SB to show stronger hands to 3‑bet). The sizing leaves plenty of stack for post‑flop play while giving meaningful fold equity. If I face a small/standard 3‑bet I’ll call and use position; if confronted with a large 3‑bet or shove I’ll re‑evaluate and likely fold to extreme aggression. Post‑flop I plan to c‑bet when appropriate and leverage position to extract value or control the pot.

2c4c is a weak suited connector with poor high-card strength and limited playability out of position. Facing a large 6BB raise from the button (Minni, who raises frequently but this sizing suggests strength), the pot odds to call 550 into 750 require ~42% equity, which this hand lacks against a decent button opening range. Postflop OOP against an aggressor would be disastrous with reverse implied odds. Flash still to act in BB, but no value in speculating here. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I am holding T2o in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 600 from the Button, which is a 6x open and larger than the usual 3x open we have seen. T2o is an extremely weak hand and does not have enough equity to justify a call, especially being out of position against the chip leader. Folding is the correct decision here to preserve my stack.

5h9c is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low high-card strength. On the button facing blinds, stealing is tempting due to position, but this hand is below standard stealing range as it's disconnected and vulnerable if called. Opponents like Minni defend wide in BB, and post-flop dynamics would be tough with reverse position risks if multiway. Pot odds to call 100 into 150 offer 3:1 but equity vs calling ranges is marginal (~30-35%), and raising risks larger pots OOP post-flop potentially. Best to fold and wait for better spots to maximize long-term stack growth.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Queen suited (Ad Qd), which is a premier starting hand in a 3-handed game. Since the Button folded, I am only up against Minni in the Big Blind. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take the initiative, as my hand is significantly ahead of a random Big Blind range.

Flash opened to 300 from the small blind. With 100 already posted in the big blind I need 200 more (total 300) to see the flop. K♥4♥ is marginal but has a king-blocker and backdoor heart potential; against a likely wide SB opening range and with deep stacks it's cheap to defend. 3-betting with this hand is unnecessary and often dominated; I'll call to see a flop and then proceed based on texture (value/raise with top pair or strong draws, fold to heavy continued aggression on dry boards).

I have Ace-Queen of diamonds on a 3h-6c-8h flop. Although I missed the flop, I maintain the initiative as the pre-flop aggressor. This board texture is somewhat coordinated, but I still hold two overcards and a backdoor straight draw. I will place a continuation bet of 325 (slightly over half-pot) to represent a strong hand and attempt to take the pot down now. If Minni calls, I still have significant equity to improve on the turn.

Board: 3♥ 6♣ 8♥. I hold K♥4♥ — a nut-heart blocker plus a two-heart flush draw and a K overcard. Flash’s flop bet is small (325 into 925) which indicates a wide c-bet range from the SB. I have 9 hearts to hit a flush (turn or river ≈35%), plus ~3 kings to pair (≈12.5%); combined chance to improve by river is roughly 45% (some of those improvements will still lose, but overall equity is strong). Calling 325 into a 925 pot gives me ~26% pot odds, which is well below my draw equity, so a call is +EV. Raising as a semi-bluff is an option, but it needlessly bloats the pot and invites a 3-bet from a deeper-stacked opponent; I prefer to realize equity cheaply and re-evaluate on the turn. Plan: call now; if I hit a heart or pair a King on the turn I will be aggressive for value; if I miss and face heavy continued aggression I will fold or proceed cautiously depending on sizing.

The turn 7h is a highly coordinated card, completing potential straights (any 5 or 9) and putting a third heart on the board for a possible flush. Since I do not hold a heart and only have overcards with no current pair or straight draw, my hand has significantly lost value. Betting here would be a risky bluff against an opponent who called the flop. Checking is the best option to control the pot size and see how Minni reacts to this dangerous board texture.

I hold Kh4h on 3h-6c-8h-7h — I’ve just made a King-high flush (only Ah beats me). Pot is 1250 and Flash (the preflop aggressor who c-bet the flop small) checked the turn; Elon (button) still to act. Against a wide SB opening/c-bet range and a caller on the button, this is a clear value spot: I want to charge worse hearts, pairs, and any turned/river draws and deny a free river to fright cards. I’ll bet for value/aggression rather than check and give initiative away. A bet of ~80% pot (1000) extracts from worse hearts and many pairs, prices out some draws, and keeps weaker hands from folding too often. If I face a large check-raise I’ll re-evaluate (raise-shove is more likely to represent Ah or a very strong hand), but absent that I expect profitable calls from worse hands — so bet 1000.

The 7 of hearts is a very dangerous card for my hand. It completes potential straights for any hand containing a 5 or a 9, and it puts a third heart on the board, making a flush possible. Minni has called both pre-flop and on the flop, suggesting she has a piece of this board or a draw that has now likely improved. My Ace-Queen high has no hearts and no straight draw. Facing a near pot-sized bet of 1000 on such a coordinated board, I have no choice but to fold and preserve my stack, as I have almost no equity and very few favorable river cards.

I am on the button with JTo in a three-handed game. This is a standard opening hand. With a stack of 7,050 (70.5 BBs), I want to play aggressively and exert pressure on the blinds. JTo has good playability post-flop and is well ahead of a random range from the small or big blind. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to take control of the pot early.

I'm in the small blind with K♥J♦ facing a button open to 300. This is a strong, playable hand that wants to take the initiative: button opens are likely fairly wide, and a 3-bet here gets fold equity, can isolate the opener, and lets me play the pot with a top face card and a decent kicker. I don't want to just call and play OOP multiway, and folding KJ is too nitty. I will make a standard sized 3-bet to 900 (≈3x the open) — big enough to charge speculative hands and make the BB pay to see a flop, but not so large as to commit me unnecessarily or induce too many all-in situations. If I'm 4-bet hard, I'll respect a very strong range and re-evaluate (likely fold to an isolating shove); if called, I'll continuation-bet favorable flops and play for value/position accordingly.

86o is a speculative hand with poor equity against the likely strong 3-bet range from Minni after Flash's button open-raise. Out of position in the BB, with two aggressive opponents, folding is standard to preserve stack for better spots. No straight potential or suited connectivity worth the 800 call into 1300 pot.

I am on the button with JTo and opened for 300. Minni, the chip leader, has 3-bet me from the small blind to 900. While JTo is a reasonable opening hand in a three-handed game, it performs poorly against a 3-bet range, especially from an aggressive chip leader who has been active in recent pots. Calling 600 more (nearly 10% of my remaining stack) puts me in a difficult position post-flop where I am likely dominated by hands like AJ, KJ, QJ, or AT. Given my current stack size relative to the other two players, I should look for better opportunities to commit chips rather than defending a marginal hand out of position in an inflated pot.

Three-handed on the button with a huge stack (≈200bb). 6♠2♦ is a very weak hand, but button open-raises very wide in 3‑handed play to steal the blinds and take the initiative. The pot is only 150 and the min-raise is 200 (2bb) — a small, low-risk attempt to pick up the blinds or play a single raised pot in position. If the BB (Flash) 3-bets large I will fold (this hand has no showdown value vs a strong 3-bet); if he calls I can use position to c-bet favorable flops or release to heavy resistance. Raising a small amount leverages fold equity while preserving my deep stack. Action: open-raise to the min (200).

T8o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity, especially out of position in the small blind facing a button raise from an aggressive player like Minni. Pot odds don't justify a call (150 to win ~500, but postflop OOP dynamics are poor), and no strong reason to raise or shove. Folding preserves stack for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Four offsuit. The button (Minni) has opened with a min-raise to 200. With 6,650 chips (66.5 BB), I have a comfortable stack to play post-flop, though I am the shortest at the table. A4o is well ahead of a standard button opening range in a 3-handed game. Given the pot odds (calling 100 to win a total pot of 350), this is an easy call. I will see a flop and evaluate from there, rather than 3-betting and potentially bloating the pot out of position against the chip leader.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace, though my kicker (4) is very weak. In the Big Blind, checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most balanced play. Given the board texture (As-Qh-7c) is quite dry, I expect the button to continuation-bet a large portion of their range. I intend to check-call to keep their bluffs in and minimize losses if I am out-kicked by a better Ace.

I opened from the button and was called by the BB, so I have position and initiative. The flop A♠ Q♥ 7♣ misses my 6♠2♦ completely and has a lot of hands in the caller’s range (A‑x, Q‑x, pocket pairs, broadways). As the preflop aggressor I should c‑bet at a high frequency to fold out missed hands and collect the pot without bloating it. I’ll use a small sizing (~1/3 pot = 150 into 450) to maximize fold equity while keeping the pot controllable if called. Plan: take it down now if he folds; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn; if he raises substantially I’ll fold given my lack of showdown equity/blockers.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace, though my kicker is weak. On a dry board like As-Qh-7c, top pair is a mandatory continue against a small 1/4 pot continuation bet (150 into 450). Minni's range for opening the button is wide, and her small bet size could represent a wide range of hands, including weaker pairs, air, or a better Ace trying to induce action. Raising here would likely only get called by better hands (stronger Aces or AQ) and would force out the bluffs I want to keep in. I will call to see the turn and keep the pot manageable, planning to re-evaluate based on the next card and her subsequent betting patterns.

I have top pair with an Ace, but my kicker is very weak. The 6d on the turn is a blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. In this situation, checking is the most prudent play. It allows me to control the pot size and gives Minni the opportunity to continue betting with her bluffs or checking back with marginal hands. Leading here doesn't accomplish much, as I'm unlikely to be called by worse or fold out better. I will check and evaluate based on Minni's response.

I picked up a pair on the turn (6♠ with 6♦ on board). Villain check‑called a small flop c‑bet, so his range is wide (Ax, Qx, pocket pairs, some broadways and marginal holdings). My 6s2d is a medium‑strength hand: it beats air/overcards and some lower pocket pairs but loses to Ax and Qx. With position and initiative, I want to extract value from worse hands and fold out pure overcards while keeping the pot controlled vs stronger holdings. A small sizing (~1/3 pot) maximizes value/fold equity and limits the cost if I’m raised. Bet 250 into 750. If he raises big I’ll fold and re‑evaluate; if he calls I’ll reassess on the river.

I have top pair with an Ace, but my kicker is weak. The 6d on the turn is a relatively blank card. Minni's bet of 250 into a 750 pot is a small, 1/3 pot-sized bet, which she could be making with a wide range of hands including any Queen, a weaker Ace, or a total bluff. Folding top pair here would be too exploitable, and raising would only serve to bloat the pot against hands that have me out-kicked (like AJ, AT, etc.) or better. Calling allows me to control the size of the pot and see a river while keeping her bluffs in her range.

I have top pair with a weak kicker on a board of As-Qh-7c-6d-Jd. Throughout the hand, I have check-called Minni's small bets to keep her range wide and avoid bloating the pot while out of position. The Jack on the river is a somewhat significant card as it connects with AJ and QJ, but it doesn't change the fact that my hand is essentially a bluff-catcher or a marginal value hand. Leading out now would likely only be called by better hands. I will check to allow Minni to check back her marginal value (like a Queen) or to bluff with any missed straight draws (like KT or T9).

I have middle pair (6♠) on A♠ Q♥ 7♣ 6♦ J♦. Flash’s line (BB call preflop, then check‑call my small flop bet and small turn bet) is consistent with a sticky, value‑heavy range (A‑x, Q‑x and pocket pairs) that largely beats my pair. A river bet will usually be called by better hands and will fold very few worse holdings, so betting is negative EV and exposes me to a raise. With position and a large stack I should pot‑control and take the showdown rather than build the pot with a marginal hand. Action: check back and go to showdown.

Preflop on the button with 5d9h offsuit, a weak speculative hand with poor connectivity and no high card strength. Pot is 150 (blinds), to call 100. While button position allows stealing blinds, 5-9o has poor postflop playability against likely calling ranges from BB (Minni deep stack) and potential SB defense (Flash medium stack). Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament with 13250 effective, deep enough to wait for premium hands. Opponent history shows Minni frequently raises preflop, suggesting she defends BB aggressively.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace of Clubs (Ac) and Four of Spades (As). Elon (the Button) has folded. The pot is 150 (100 BB + 50 SB), and it's 50 more for me to call into Minni, who is in the Big Blind. Given the current stack sizes (7350 for me, 19250 for Minni), and the fact that we are down to 3 players, Ace-high is a strong starting hand in this scenario. Completing the small blind or raising are both standard. Raising is generally better to take control and potentially win the pot pre-flop. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on Minni and assert strength.

I’m in the big blind with A♣T♣ against a small-blind open to 300 (pot 400, 200 to call). Heads-up ATo suited is a strong hand, but calling keeps the opener’s positional advantage. I want to isolate the opener, gain fold equity and build a pot with a hand that plays well postflop, so I’ll 3-bet to a standard size (~2.5–3x the open). Raising to 900 pressures wide SB opens, narrows their continuing range, and gives me a clear plan postflop; if I face a large 4-bet I’ll re-evaluate.fold equity and stack depths make this a profitable, controlled aggression spot.

I opened from the Small Blind with Ace-Four offsuit, and Minni (the Big Blind and chip leader) has responded with a 3-bet to 900. In a three-handed game, Ace-high hands have value, but calling a 3-bet out of position with a weak kicker against an aggressive chip leader is often a losing play. Minni has shown significant aggression in recent hands (Hands 92, 93, 94). Given my stack size relative to the blinds, I don't want to bleed chips in a marginal spot where I will likely be forced to fold on the flop if I don't improve. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit a significant portion of my stack.

I am on the button with Queen-Ten offsuit in a three-handed game. This hand is well within the standard opening range for this position. I will raise to 300 to attempt to take down the blinds or play the pot in position if called. While I am currently the shortest stack at the table, I still have 71 big blinds, which allows for plenty of post-flop play.

Pocket jacks (Jd Jc) in the small blind vs a button open to 300 is a premium spot to 3-bet. Calling 250 leaves me multiway and out of position; that reduces my postflop edge. I want to 3-bet for value and fold equity to isolate the button and charge speculative callers from the big blind. I size to 1000 (about 3.3x the open): large enough to get value and deny cheap odds to draws, but small relative to my 19,600 stack so I can comfortably fold to a large 4-bet or continue postflop if called. If I face a huge 4-bet I will re-evaluate (likely fold unless it's an all-in by the short stack); if called I will proceed cautiously out of position, looking to realize equity on favorable flops.

Preflop in the big blind with KTo offsuit facing a button open to 300 from Flash and a 3-bet to 1000 from Minni in the small blind. KTo is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position postflop against a likely strong 3-bet range from the SB. Pot odds to call 900 into 1400 are about 1.55:1, but implied odds are limited with offsuit broadway and two aggressive opponents. Minni has shown frequent preflop aggression across hands (e.g., Hand 51, 52, 54, etc.), suggesting a polarized range including premiums and bluffs, but defending wide OOP is -EV long-term in this deep-stack tournament phase (53 hands left). Folding preserves our 13k stack for better spots while maximizing EV over the tournament.

I am on the button with QTo and have been 3-bet by Minni for the second consecutive hand. While Minni appears to be aggressively defending the blinds and attacking my opens, QTo is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by a standard 3-betting range (AQ, KQ, QJ, and any pocket pair). Although I have position, calling a 3.3x 3-bet with this hand would put me in a difficult spot post-flop. Given that I still have 68 big blinds, I will fold and look for a better opportunity to make a stand or 4-bet with a stronger range rather than bleeding chips with mediocre hands against the chip leader.

I’m on the button with Jh6d in a 3-handed game. This hand is marginal, but being in position gives me a lot of post‑flop leverage and a good chance to pick up the blinds. The min‑raise is 200, but my standard open size at this table has been ~300 (3x the BB), which achieves two things: it gives me more fold equity to steal the blinds and it reduces the frequency and profitability of light 3‑bets from the blinds. I have a deep stack relative to the blinds, so a 300 open is low risk and keeps postflop play manageable if called. Plan: open to 300 to either take the pot preflop or play a single‑raised pot in position; fold to a sizable 3‑bet since J6o fares poorly against strong 3‑bet ranges; if called I’ll c‑bet favorable, dry flops and proceed cautiously when I miss.

A4 offsuit from the small blind facing a button raise to 300 (250 to call into 450 pot). Pot odds require ~26% equity, but A4o plays poorly OOP postflop: ace often dominated, low kicker issues with pair, poor flush/straight draws. Minni raises frequently but shows strength in pots. Folding preserves stack (131 BB deep) for premium hands; no fold equity from raise profitable here vs loose opponent. Standard fold.

In 3-handed play, ATo is a very strong hand, especially defending the Big Blind against a Button open. Minni has been quite active with her opening range. I will 3-bet to 1000 to take initiative and build a pot with a hand that likely dominates her opening range, while also giving her a chance to fold pre-flop.

I opened to 300 from the button with Jh6d and Flash (BB) 3-bet to 1000. The pot is 1350 and it’s 700 more to call (1000 total), so I’d need roughly 34% equity to break even. J6o is frequently dominated by a typical BB 3-bet range and plays poorly post‑flop even in position. A small 4‑bet won’t fold out enough of his range and a shove is an unnecessarily high‑variance play against a fairly committed effective stack (~5800). Preserving my 19.7k stack and waiting for a better spot is the correct long‑term decision here — fold.

3-handed on the button with KQs, a premium hand that plays well post-flop in position. Pot has 150 from blinds, facing a to-call of 100, likely limped pot or small action. Raising for value against wide calling ranges from blinds, to build pot with strong equity, steal dead money, and gain initiative. Standard sizing around 4x BB given dynamics. Effective stacks allow aggression without overcommitting.

With 6c 7c in the Small Blind, I am facing a 4x open from Elon on the Button. While suited connectors have good playability, calling here would put me out of position against a deep-stacked aggressive opponent for the rest of the hand. Given that I am currently the shortest stack at the table (71BB), I prefer to avoid marginal spots out of position against the larger stacks unless I have a hand with more high-card strength or a clear opportunity to 3-bet. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities where I have positional advantage.

I’m in the big blind with Qc9c facing Elon’s button open to 400. I’ve already posted 100, so it’s 300 more to call (total 400). The pot is 550, so calling 300 requires ~35–37% equity — Q9s fares well enough vs a standard button opening range and the deep stacks give good implied odds to realize equity. A 3‑bet is an option, but the min-raise to 700 gives little fold equity and a larger 3‑bet would unnecessarily bloat the pot out of position and risk a 4‑bet from an aggressive opponent. I’ll call to keep the pot manageable, see a flop, and proceed based on texture (continue aggressively with club draws/top pair, control pot or fold on dry/hostile boards).

Flop is A♠ A♦ 3♣; I have Q♣9♣ — no pair and only a runner‑runner club flush (needs both turn+river clubs, ≈4%). This is a very bad texture for my hand: the preflop raiser’s range contains many A‑x and strong hands that now beat me. I’m out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with a weak hand or attempt a marginal bluff on a dry, paired board. I’ll check to control the pot and preserve my stack; plan is to fold to any meaningful c‑bet (only entertain a call to a tiny probe if the sizing gives proper odds for the long backdoor draw or if I pick up a strong turn). If Elon checks back I’ll reassess on the turn and only apply pressure on genuinely favorable cards.

Paired aces flop (As Ad 3c) is very dry, rainbow, few draws. Heads-up vs BB caller after my button raise to 400. Minni checked, giving me initiative as preflop raiser. KQs has no pair or draw but blocks some broadway; standard continuation bet for thin value (vs weak aces/pairs), protection (minimal draws), and fold equity vs air/marginal hands in her wide BB calling range. She folds often to aggression postflop in history (e.g., many folded to bets). Position allows control. Bet ~60% pot to balance value/protection. Pot 850, bet 500 puts pressure without overbetting. Stacks deep, but this builds pot when ahead or takes it down.

I checked the flop to control the pot with Qc9c on A♠ A♦ 3♣. I have no pair and only a runner‑runner club flush (~4% to hit by river). Elon was the button preflop and is representing a very strong continuing range (many A‑x, sets, strong pairs) — his 500 bet into the 1350 pot is a meaningful c‑bet. Calling 500 into a 1350 pot requires about 27% equity (500 / (1350+500) ≈ 27%), which my hand does not have against his range. While I have some backdoor and pairing chances (≈24% to pair a Q/9 by river), those often remain second best to any Ace in his hand. A check‑raise bluff OOP would be high variance and unlikely to fold enough of his range here. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 9-4 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. My stack size of 7100 (71 BBs) is sufficient to wait for a better spot. Both Minni (SB) and Elon (BB) have shown significant aggression in recent hands, frequently 3-betting or defending their blinds. Attempting to steal the pot with such a weak hand into active opponents is not a high-value play. I will fold and wait for a stronger opening opportunity.

Flash (button) folded — heads-up vs Elon in the big blind. I have 9♦8♦, a strong suited connector for heads-up play with deep stacks (19,300). Limping gives Elon a free look and concedes initiative; folding is too passive. I'll open-raise to the minimum standard (to 200) to apply pressure, isolate the BB, and build the pot with a hand that plays well postflop (flush/straight potential). This size gives good fold equity against Elon’s defending range while keeping maneuverability postflop; if he 3-bets big I can re-evaluate (likely fold or proceed cautiously), if he calls I’ll continue with position-awareness and look to realize equity on connected/diamond boards.

Qd2s is a trash hand: offsuit, poor connectivity, dominated by any Qx or better, low playability OOP vs SB range. SB raises are wide (often 40-60%+), but Q2o equity is ~35-40% vs that range, and postflop OOP it's hard to realize. Calling 100 into 300 (pot odds ~4:1, needing ~20% equity) is marginal at best, but solvers defend tighter with offsuit low Qx here deepstacked. History shows Minni aggressive but pot odds don't justify spew; fold to preserve stack for better spots in 50 remaining hands.

I’m on the button with 7♠5♣ — a weak but playable hand in position and the blinds are the only players left. With deep stacks (my stack ~194bb) and two opponents (SB Elon 134bb, BB Flash 70bb), I want to seize the initiative and apply fold equity rather than limp and play OOP. A standard 2x BB open to 200 is a small, low-variance steal that keeps the pot manageable but forces the blinds to defend. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call or flat small defenses and play postflop in position, c-bet favorable flops and control pot size on bad boards.

6d3c is a complete trash hand: offsuit, low cards, disconnected, no straight or flush potential worth chasing. Facing a button raise to 200 (2BB), which is standard and wide-ranging but still strong enough. From SB, calling 150 more OOP with this is -EV, as I'll be out of position postflop with minimal playability. No fold equity for a squeeze given villain stacks (Minni 192BB deep, Flash 70BB) and my own deep stack. Best to fold and wait for better spots to maximize stack over 150 hands.

I hold AsKs, which is a premium starting hand. Minni's open from the button represents a wide range of hands, many of which are dominated by Ace-King. I am 3-betting to 700 for value and to take control of the hand. This sizing builds the pot and exerts pressure on the button opener while still allowing me space to navigate post-flop with my 7,000 chip stack.

I opened to 200 on the button with 7♠5♣ and the big blind 3-bet to 700 (pot 950). Calling 500 to play postflop in position would require ~25–30% equity (to win a ~1450 pot) against a 3-bet range — but 7-5 offsuit is thin, often dominated, and plays poorly in multi-street pots versus a decent 3-bettor. A 4-bet bluff to 1200 is too speculative with this holding and will put unnecessary chips at risk. I’ll fold, preserve my deep stack, and wait for a better spot to use position or apply pressure.

AJoff is a strong hand from the button in an unopened pot against blinds. With position on both opponents, this is a standard spot to raise for value and to thin the field. Minni has a large stack and is aggressive, Flash shorter but still deep. Raising to 3x the BB (300 total) is standard sizing to build the pot while maintaining fold equity. No history this hand suggests limp-trapping.

I am in the Small Blind with 3c 7h, which is a very weak hand. Elon has raised from the button. Given my stack size and the poor quality of my cards, there is no reason to defend or contest this pot out of position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Facing a button open to 300 with Q♥9♦ in the big blind. Pot is 450 and it costs me 200 more to call (total commitment after calling = 300). Q9o is playable heads-up — it has decent equity, a queen blocker and some straight/paired-up potential. Elon has been raising frequently, so his range is fairly wide; given deep stacks and the cheap price to see a flop, I should defend rather than fold or make a marginal 3-bet. I won’t 3‑bet OOP with Q9o (too weak for a value 3‑bet and not a good bluff candidate here). Plan postflop: see the flop, proceed with caution — continue with top pair, good draws or favorable textures, fold to heavy pressure on dry boards when missed. Call 200 more (total 300 committed).

Flop A♣ J♥ 8♠ and I hold Q♥9♦ (queen-high). I called preflop to defend the BB and planned to proceed cautiously postflop. This board strongly favors the preflop raiser — lots of A‑ and J‑combos in his range — and my holdings are just a one-card gutshot to a T plus pairing outs (3 queens + 3 nines). While I have ~10 outs to improve to at least a pair by the river (~38% to hit), many of those improvements (Q/9) can still be behind an A or J. I have no flush and no two-card straight draw, and I'm out of position. With deep stacks and no bet yet, I want to keep the pot small and gain information. I'll check to see how Elon reacts: call small c-bets (good pot odds + equity), fold to very large aggression, and play more aggressively only if I improve (Q/9/10) or pick up clear fold equity.

Top two pair (Aces and Jacks) with top kicker on a flop of 8s Jh Ac, which is a dream board for my Ad Js. This hits perfectly: pair of Aces using board Ac, pair of Jacks using board Jh and my Js. No strong draws complete (possible straight draws like 9T, but unlikely; spade flush draw possible but board has only one spade). Minni called pre-flop raise from BB, suggesting a wide defensive calling range including suited connectors, Ax, KQ, etc., many of which hit middle pair, bottom pair, or draws here. She checked, which is standard but weak. With position and deep stacks (my 13100 effective vs her 18950), betting for value and protection is optimal. Half-pot sizing (approx 325) extracts from worse hands like AT, AJ weaker, KQ, 89, flush draws, etc., while denying equity to draws. History shows Minni calls bets frequently (e.g., Hand 29, 56, 60) and is aggressive but here passive so far. Builds pot for turn without overcommitting.

I checked the flop with Q♥9♦ to see how Elon would proceed. He c-bet small (450 into a 1100 pot ≈41%), and he’s shown a tendency to raise/pressure often — his range here is wide (Ax, Jx, many missed broadway combos). I have 6 clean outs (3 Queens + 3 Nines) — ~24% to pair by the river. Calling 450 costs me 450 to win a 1550 pot (pot odds ≈29%), slightly worse than raw equity but acceptable given his wide range, the small bet size, deep stacks, and the chance to realize implied equity or fold out air hands on later streets. I won’t 3‑bet OOP with Q9o — it’s a marginal hand and a poor bluff candidate here. Plan: call to see the turn (call 450 more, total committed this hand = 750). If I miss the turn I’ll fold to heavy turn aggression; if I pair a Q or 9 (or pick up a strong improvement), I’ll proceed more aggressively and extract value.

I missed the turn — the J on the turn pairs the board (A♣ J♥ 8♠ J♣) and my Q♥9♦ has not improved. Elon is an aggressive, wide preflop raiser and his range contains many AJ/Jx/strong A-high combos that now beat me. I’m out of position and stacks are deep, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand that has only ~6 clean outs and a lot of dominated outs. Best line is pot control: check and fold to meaningful aggression. If Elon checks back I can see a cheap river; if he bets I’ll fold to medium/large bets and only consider calling very small offers that give me correct pot odds.

Turn pairs the Jacks on the board (Jh and now Jc), upgrading my hand from top two pair to the nuts: trip Jacks (Js with board Jh/Jc) and Ace kicker (Ad with Ac). This is an incredibly strong hand, likely the best possible given the paired board. Minni's range after calling preflop raise and flop c-bet: Ax (weaker like A8-A10), flush draws (spade heavy board start), straight draws (9T, T9), KQ, or bluffs, but her double check suggests weakness or trap though history shows her passive when behind. No Flash. Deep stacks (my 12650 eff vs her 18500), pot 1550. With position, value bet to extract from worse Ax, draws, two-pair (A8, J8 etc.). 70% pot sizing (1100) balances value/protection, induces calls from her loose tendencies (e.g., Hand 29 full river call, Hand 56 massive calls), denies equity to any missed draws. Avoid check to pot control as she may have showdown value or check-raise bluff.

Board A♣ J♥ 8♠ J♣, my Q♥9♦. I checked the turn and Elon bet 1,100 into a 2,650 pot — I must call 1,100. I only have 6 outs (3 Queens + 3 Nines) to hit the river (~13% equity). Calling 1,100 to win 2,650 requires ~29% equity (1,100 / (2,650+1,100) ≈ 29%). My equity is far below the pot-odds required. Elon is an aggressive raiser with a range that includes many A- and J-combinations that already dominate my outs (and a Q or 9 often still loses to an Ace). Out of position and facing meaningful aggression on the turn, this is a clear fold — preserve 18,500 chips for better, more +EV spots.

I am on the button in a three-handed game with Jack-Ten offsuit. This is a premium opening hand in this position. I have approximately 73 big blinds, which is a healthy stack but shorter than both opponents. Opening to 3x the big blind is standard to take control of the pot early and exert pressure on the blinds, especially since I will have position throughout the hand.

Facing a button open to 300 from the button with Q♠4♠ in the small blind. This is a marginal but defendable suited queen — it has flush/backdoor straight potential and good implied odds with deep stacks. Folding is too nitty vs a BTN open; a light 3-bet/raise is unnecessary and would commit me out of position with a weak hand that is often dominated. I’ll call the 250 (300 total committed) to see a flop and play cautiously: if the BB jams or I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold, otherwise proceed with aggression only on favourable flops (flush/strong pair/draw).

Td3s is a complete trash hand, offsuit, disconnected, poor playability. Facing a button raise from Flash and a call from Minni in SB, pot is 700, calling 200 OOP as BB. Pot odds are decent (~3.5:1) but equity realization postflop will be terrible out of position with this hand. No strong blockers or fold equity for a raise/shove. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots. Long-term EV positive.

I have top pair (Q♠) but a very weak 4 kicker and I’m out of position vs the button who opened preflop. Flash’s opening range contains lots of Kx and Ax that beat me, plus some worse Qx and bluffs I can extract value from. Pot control is important — I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a vulnerable kicker. I’ll check and let the button have to prove it: call small-to-medium continuation bets to get value from worse Qs/small pairs and check back/clr the turn if he checks. If he jams or puts in a very large raise representing a strong K/A/full range I’ll fold, since I’m often behind.

I have flopped an open-ended straight draw (J-T on a K-Q-2 board). Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and have position, a continuation bet is the most effective play here. This board texture heavily favors my perceived range (which includes many Kings and Queens), and a bet can often force a fold from hands like small pairs or weaker draws that Minni might have called with from the small blind. If called, I still have significant equity to improve on the turn.

Flop: Qc Kh 2h. I hold Q♠4♠ — top pair but a very weak kicker and I’m out of position vs the BTN opener. Flash’s bet of 400 into the ~700 pot is a small-to-medium c-bet that represents a wide range (Kx, Ax, some Qx, pocket pairs and bluffs). I want to control the pot OOP and extract value from worse Qx/small pairs and draws rather than inflate the pot with a marginal kicker. Calling 400 gives me good pot odds (~27%), keeps weaker hands and bluffs in, and lets me reevaluate on the turn. Plan: call now, check back if he checks the turn, call small/standard turn bets for value/protection, and fold to heavy turn/river aggression that polarizes his range (big raises/jams).

I have Q♠4♠ on Q♣ K♥ 2♥ Q♥ — that means I’ve turned trips (three queens). This is a very strong hand, but it’s vulnerable to full houses/quads (pocket Kings, pocket 2s, or the remaining queen). Flash opened from the button and c‑bet the flop, so his range contains lots of Kx/Ax, some Qx, medium pairs and bluffs. Out of position, I want to maximize value while not scaring off Kx/weak pairs that will pay me off. Checking the turn is the best line: it induces bluffs and small/medium bets from hands that currently beat or call a bet (Kx, worse Qx, draws), and lets me either raise/shove those bets for value or extract more on the river if he checks back. Plan: check now; if Flash bets small–medium I’ll raise (often shove) to get his stack; if he jams huge representing a polarized full‑house range I’ll reassess but will frequently call because many of his calling hands to a raise are worse. If he checks back I’ll take a river‑line to get additional value or control the pot depending on the card. Therefore: check to induce.

The turn card is the Queen of hearts, pairing the board. I currently have Jack-high with an open-ended straight draw (straight outs: Ace or Nine). Minni has checked to me after calling my flop continuation bet. Given the board has paired and I have significant equity with my straight draw, checking behind is a strong tactical play. It allows me to realize my equity for free and see the river without risking a check-raise from Minni, who has a much larger stack. If I hit my straight on the river, my hand will be well-disguised, and I can then go for value.

I have trips (Q♠) on Qc Kh 2h Qh Tc — a very strong but not invulnerable hand. Flash (BTN) c‑bet the flop and then checked the turn, which looked like weakness or a trap; either way I induced and said I would take a river line for value. There are full‑house combos that beat me (pocket Ks, 2s, pocket Ts and the lone Qd combined with K/2/T) but those combinations are limited. Flash’s opening and flop line contain a lot of Kx/Ax and worse Qx that will still pay a medium river price. Checking risks letting him check back (losing value) or bet small and fold out worse hands on a check/raise — betting first extracts value and avoids giving a cheap opportunity for him to bluff‑jam. I want a size that gets called by Kx and worse Qx but doesn’t over‑charge the calling range, so I’ll bet ~2/3–3/4 pot. If he raises big (polarized), I’ll reassess — large river raises often represent the relatively few full houses and I’ll proceed cautiously (likely fold to an all‑in raise unless the sizing/reaction suggests a thin bluff).

On the river, the board is Qc-Kh-2h-Qh-Tc, giving me two pair (Queens and Tens with a King kicker). While I improved on the river, this card is extremely coordinated. It completes several straights (AJ and J9) and any King in Minni's range (which is wide after calling from the small blind and calling a flop continuation bet) now has a superior two pair (Kings and Queens). Minni's lead of 1,200 into a 1,500 pot is a very strong polarizing action. Given that she called twice and then led out big on a card that completes many draws and improves many 'made' hands that already beat me, I believe her range is heavily weighted toward value. My hand is merely a bluff-catcher here, and against such a large sizing on this board texture, a fold is the most disciplined and stack-preserving play.

Button with 3♠5♣ is a weak hand but we have full positional advantage vs only the blinds and very deep stacks. Limping (call 100) invites a multi‑way pot with poor equity; folding surrenders easy steal opportunities. I’ll open‑raise the minimum to 200 to apply pressure and pick up the pot preflop or play last postflop with initiative. If either blind 3‑bets I’ll fold this holding; if called I’ll c‑bet on dry flops and proceed cautiously on coordinated boards.

8s2c is one of the worst possible starting hands: low, offsuit, disconnected. Facing a button raise to 200 from Minni, who raises frequently based on history. Pot odds are poor (calling 150 into 350, ~30% equity needed, but this hand has <20% vs typical raising range). No speculative value postflop. Deep stacks don't justify playing trash from out of position. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I am in the Big Blind with 92o, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Minni has raised to 200 from the Button. Although it is only 100 more for me to call into a pot of 350, providing decent pot odds, the hand performs very poorly post-flop and I will be out of position against the chip leader. With 65 big blinds, I have plenty of space to wait for a better spot rather than defending with trash.

Unraised pot on the button in a 3-handed situation with K6o. King-high has decent blocker and playability postflop from position. Opponents have large stacks but history shows frequent preflop folds to button raises. Standard steal attempt: raise to 3x the big blind to take down the blinds without committing too much.

Elon is opening frequently from the button, but Q8o is a weak hand to defend out of the small blind. With the chip leader (Minni) still to act in the big blind and likely to call or put on pressure, playing this hand out of position is a losing proposition. As the shortest stack, I prefer to fold and preserve my chips for a more advantageous situation.

Heads-up vs Elon (button) who opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with K♠5♥ — a marginal but defendable hand in the BB. The pot is 450 and I need to put in 200 more (300 total), giving me ~30.8% break-even equity. Versus a typical wide button opening range K5o has enough equity to justify a call. I’ll avoid a light 3-bet here: raising to 500 would put me deeper in with a poorly playable hand out of position and reduce my maneuverability postflop. With deep stacks I can realize equity and play postflop (check/fold or pot-control) if I miss, and continue aggressively if I flop a king or good draw. Plan: call to see the flop, evaluate texture and opponent’s tendencies, fold to overwhelming aggression on bad boards but pursue value when I connect.

I flopped top pair (K♠) after defending the big blind to Elon’s button open. This is exactly the scenario I planned to pursue value on — Elon's opening range contains a lot of Kx and weaker pairs that will call a modest bet. There are no dangerous draw-heavy textures to punish a half‑pot c-bet, so I want to extract value and deny cheap cards. I’ll make a ~1/2 pot bet (300 into 650) — small enough to get called by worse kings and medium pairs, large enough to price out some equity and keep the pot controlled. If Elon raises big, I’ll fold most of the time given my weak kicker; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously on scary runouts.

Flop is 2d-2h-Kc, paired dry board. I have top pair (Kd) with a weak kicker but good position heads-up. Minni's small 300 bet into 950 pot suggests weakness: possible weaker Kx, Ax, or air/draws, as strong hands like sets or 22 might check or bet larger. Preflop she defended wide from BB vs button raise. Raising protects against turn cards that complete draws (e.g., 3x for trips, flush/ straight draws unlikely but possible) and gets value from worse. Size to 3x her bet (900 total) balances value and fold equity while keeping pot manageable deepstacked. Excellent pot odds if called, continue aggression on favorable turns.
